image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment: From the graph, I noticed that legacy applicants have a significantly higher admissions rate across the board (over twice the average rate). However, those wither higher parental income had a proportionally larger benefit: up to 7 times the average for the top 0.1%, compared to less than triple (already high) for non-legacy. This leaves several burning questions: among them, why are admissions so heavily skewed in favor of high-income, legacy applicants, and what steps can colleges take to reduce this (likely-systematic) bias?

Legacy applicants have a significantly higher admissions rate across the board.
Legacy applicants have an admissions rate that is over twice the average rate.
Those with higher parental income had a proportionally larger benefit.
The benefit is up to 7 times the average for the top 0.1%.
The benefit is less than triple for non-legacy.
This leaves several burning questions.
One question is why admissions are so heavily skewed in favor of high-income legacy applicants.
One question is what steps colleges can take to reduce this likely-systematic bias.

raw_comment: It's obvious that legacy applicants have a greater chance at getting a perfect sear on their college admissions steak compared to those lighting up the stove with their average admissions match. What I find most boggling is the same thing the article finds boggling: if colleges perpetuate privilege. I didn't even think this was a thing before. But this doesn't concern me too much as I don't have the brain nor dough for Harvard. That said, it is an eye-opener to learn how "unfair" it is.

raw_comment: I noticed that students with a higher income and legacy parents were more likely to be admitted. This could be for a variety of factors such as the fact that hire income families don't require scholarships and have access to more tutoring and educational resources, therefore getting better grades and more likely to give the school mor money. These reasons should not cause an equally hard working student with less resources, less connections, and less money, but of the same intelligence to not be admitted. Unfortunately this is not the case. It is also important to note that statistics can be very easily skewed and data is hard to collect 100% accurately so we still do not know the exact statistics and factors that go into these decisions.

raw_comment:
When observing the graph of college admissions, you can see that kids with a legacy boost are at a much more significant advantage compared to kids who do not have a legacy. 
We can also see that even kids with the highest income level are at an enormous disadvantage compared to kids with a higher income and a legacy boost. 
This is disheartening because as a high achieving and striving student who wants to attend an Ivy League just because I don't have a legacy, I am already behind before applying. It also makes me even more upset for the students already at an economic disadvantage because even if they are much better academically than another student with a legacy, the lesser student is more likely to be accepted because of the school's predisposed preference.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
Looking at the graph, I can see that, for legacy applicants, applications of those with a family income rank of more than the 50th percentile essentially stays at a rate of 2.5 times as likely or more to be accepted. However, I'm noticing that non-legacies with an income rank of about 85% are under 1 times as likely to be accepted into an elite college. 

After analyzing the graph, I wonder how these admissions numbers will be impacted over the next several years. After the Supreme Court case in June that eliminated affirmative action in terms of college admissions on the grounds of things like race, I'm curious to see how this will extend to legacy statuses. Legacies aren't necessarily considered to be disadvantaged, but non-legacies may be, so it'll be interesting to see if legacy admissions rise or drop. 

This graph doesn't necessarily affect my immediate community very much, as I don't know anyone who was aiming to be accepted into a highly selective college. However, I do live in the general proximity of Rice University, which is a very selective college that likely exhibits admissions bias based on whether someone is a legacy and the income bracket that they are in. 

A headline that captures the graph's main idea might be "Application Distortion: Legacies vs. Everyone Else."

raw_comment: From the first glance I noticed how significant of a role  your admission rate is impacted by being a legacy applicant whose parent's income falls under 99%. The chances are significantly higher even for kids whose parents' income does not fall in the top 99%. Another thing that I noticed was for those who fell under the category of parents income between the 70th and 90th percentile actually had a lower admission rate than those who had slightly lower and higher income. I wonder why the mixture of being a legacy and your parents income being within the 99 percentile increases your chance far greater than any other combination shown in the graph. I see others around me in highschool spending countless hours perfecting their craft, studying till the break of dawn, volunteering and starting impactful foundations for their chances to be super seeded by those with legacy and their parents having being in a higher income range. A catchy headlines could be "Silver Spoon Scholars: How legacy and Parental Wealth shines in College Admissions".

What do you notice? I noticed that being a legacy applicant significantly increased the student’s chances of being admitted into the school. So, it seems that schools care deeply about passing on the legacy of attending their school.
What do you wonder? I wonder why being in the 70 to 90th percentile decreases the chance of being accepted. Your parents obviously have more money to afford the school. Why would the school decrease the number of students in the income bracket?
How does this relate to you and your community? As a gymnast, who is not a legacy applicant to the colleges I would like to attend, I am trying to get admitted using a non-traditional way by being recruited for gymnastics. This would allow me to circumvent many issues like not being a legacy applicant.

raw_comment: It was interesting to see how much a student's legacy can effect their acceptance rate, especially for students of parent's with higher incomes. For students of parents who have a mid to high income, legacy has a significant boost on their acceptance rates. I wonder why legacy is so important to these colleges. Parents and their children can differ significantly in character traits and personalities, which makes the idea of legacy a questionable way to admit students. If these colleges' goal is to attract and accept the most intelligent, talented, and skilled students, why is such an emphasis placed on legacy? It seems irresponsible of these colleges to accept based on legacy and unfair to the multitude of highly talented students who are rejected because they lack legacy.

This relates to me because as a Junior, I need to start looking for colleges. Not being a legacy applicant will affect my options greatly. A catchy headline for this could be: Status over Smarts?: Is being a legacy applicant more important than being academically smart?

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
I noticed that for legacy applicants, they are 7x as likely to be admitted as opposed to applicants without legacy status, meaning that if an applicant had relatives with a history of attending a certain elite university such as Ivy Leagues, then they had a much higher chance of being accepted compared to an applicant who did not have any prior legacy status in their family. Also, the richer an applicant's parents' income rank was and the more upper class they were, the higher the chance they were to be admitted into an elite college, even under the scenario of students with the same test scores. 

I wonder why it's harder for applicants without legacy status to be admitted into the same university with the same scores as others who have family or are a apart of generations in their family that have a history of attending the elite college that they are currently applying too. While I do understand why generations of a family might wish to all attend the same prestigious school, I don't think it's fair to other applicants who work just as hard but are simply the first of their family to apply and wish to be admitted. However, I think that this current statistic very likely has to do with elite colleges wishing to keep their students "elite", and the fact that most legacy status applicants most likely come from wealthy families that are able to use connections and legacy history to their advantage. 

Pt. 1- Questions 1 & 2 (My response was too long sorry :D)

raw_comment:
This relates to me and my community because I'm currently a junior, soon-to-be rising senior, who's likely going to start having to apply to colleges later this year, and I am by no means a legacy applicant or of legacy status, and neither are many of my current acquaintances. I can say that I'm already fortunate enough to have grown up privileged and never "poor", but nevertheless, I am far from "rich" in terms of income rank, and according to this graph, that will also unfortunately affect my chances of being accepted into more elite and prestigious colleges. 

Catchy Headline: Smarts or Status?: Advantages of Legacy & Affluent Applicants 

Pt. 2- Questions 3 & 4

raw_comment:
Apart from the stand-out jump in the admission rate for legacy applicants of the 99.9% income percentile, I found it interesting that the admissions rate was higher for non-legacy applicants of the <50% income percentile than it were for the 50-95% higher income percentile of those applying. Interestingly enough, the admissions rate for the same income brackets of legacy applicants do not follow this trend but rather continually get higher until reaching the extremes.
I wonder of what the admissions graph would look like if instead of using the income percentiles, the graph used fixed yearly salaries. According to what I saw in the bottom of the page, the 99 or 99.9 percentiles represented those with an income of 620k+, I would be interested to see how admissions rates change in the millions or tens/hundreds of millions range. I believe that this graph relates to Texas in the way that many students in this state, regardless of socioeconomic status, do not even apply to out-of-state colleges in the first place. Many students are happy to stick with applying with their regional legacy schools. As being from an immigrant family in Australia with no prior experiences of the American College system, I find it interesting regarding the state boundaries that many confine themselves to as the equivalent state/province boundaries in Australia are nowhere near as considered. For a title: "Elite Admissions Opportunities Impacted by Socio-Economic Status."

raw_comment:
It doesn't seem to have occurred to anyone that the most prestigious colleges are that way because of who they admit, and changing that is not going to give an advantage to those who were formerly not admitted, but will simply make these schools no longer prestigious.

That is, it is plausible that Harvard graduates are desirable because Harvard screens its applicants for desirability, not because of the education they get once they are there. It's a similar fallacy for all highly selective schools - if a school only allows in the best students (for whatever meaning of best you like), the school and it's teachers don't have to be any good, because the best students will excel regardless.

raw_comment: I am sick and tired of hearing about the Ivy League. For the last 47 years, I have taught at one of the top public unis in the world and I have had grad students from the Ivy League. Their privilege has been nauseating. They have had so many things handed to them and can not work well in health care. On average, they have been sheltered and can not tolerate stress well. I find it especially amusing that Duke, or Fluke, is mentioned here. Many of their faculty live hand to mouth, on grant funding. The school is so cheap, it will not even pay their salaries. It breeds an unhealthy environment, to say nothing of  insufferable humidity.

raw_comment:
I noticed that applicants who come from a higher income background and have a legacy status have higher chances of acceptance into the universities. 
I wonder how this process affects the acceptance rates of applicants who come from a lower income background and also without a legacy status. 
This relates to my community and I because we share the same belief that the acceptance process should have an equal chance for all applicants, no matter their financial background or legacy status. 
My catchy headline for the graph is "Prepare for the Unfair Truth Behind College Admissions".

raw_comment: Something that immediately sticks out to the reader is the greater income the greater the chance is to be accepted. The question on my mind is, do we understand the effect of this habit? ### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that those who come from higher income families are more likely to get accepted into elite colleges unlike those who come from poor families. From the graph, I can see that families with a legacy are also more likely to to get accepted.
2. I wonder does this contribute to a greater cycle of higher income students who are able to get into elite colleges while those who come from poor families have a low chance of getting accepted. Is there any way to stop the cycle?
3. This relates to me and my community because I feel like everyone should have an equal opportunity to get into elite colleges. There are people who would love to attend college but can’t due to the high costs or lack of resources. 
4. A name for the graph could be “College Admissions: An Unfair Advantage”

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that legacy applicants had at least a doubled chance of being admitted and as parents' income rank increase so did the chance of admission. However, I noticed that the students whose parents had an income rank that was in the 50th rank or the middle rank had less of a chance of being admitted into the college.

2. I wonder why the legacy students' whose parents' income rank was in the middle of the rank or in the 50th rank had less of a chance of admission than the other legacy students with a lower or higher rank. Is this graph telling us that there is a possible abundance of legacy students with parents who are in the 50th income rank? Are people in the middle class experiencing some form of discrimination in the college process? Are we experiencing injustice or high demand?

3. This relates to my community in a great deal of ways. I am currently in high school and I plan to go to college. I am aware that college isn't the goal for all high school students, but I know that many students future plans include going to college. The people I surround myself with are mostly all academically motivated and are all trying very hard to make themselves appealing to colleges. We constantly strive to get good grades and fill our schedules with clubs and extra curriculars, while simultaneously keeping a social life. We are in high school after all.

4. ARE THE CHANCES OF BEING ADMITTED TO COLLEGE CONNECTED TO INCOME AND LEGACY?

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
I noticed that the acceptance rate for legacy applicants go up by a lot, compared to applicants that are not legacy. 

I wonder how this data was found. What is this based off? What are the "some elite colleges" mentioned? Does extracurriculars and grades affect this graph (not just test scores)? 

This relates to me and my community because I am in High School and will be applying to colleges soon. Also, both my parents attended a top 20 college in the US so I am wondering if that would help my chances at getting in. My understanding is no because both my older siblings applied and did not get in.

raw_comment:
I've found that, compared to typical students, Legacy students have an approximately twofold higher chance of being accepted into Elite universities. Additionally, I saw that the graph fell at the 85th percentile, but then it shot up to seven times higher acceptance rates. Why does the graph dip at about the 85th percentile, I wonder? In addition, I'm curious as to why, up until approximately the 50th percentile, neither graph changes significantly; after that, they start to diverge until the 99th percentile. In my neighborhood, I can relate to this because I'm a high school student approaching the age when students need to start thinking seriously about college. Resources like these assist students understand the admission rates to different universities.

raw_comment:
1) People who fall between the 70th and 90th percentiles, in my opinion, are not average. The legacy is more likely to be accepted and provides an advantage. 
2)I'm curious how the graph might vary based on the year it was created. Additionally, I'm curious as to why the income 10th percentile is above normal while the 75th to 90th percentiles are below.
3)Since there aren't many extremely wealthy people in our area—roughly 99.5%—I believe that being accepted into a prestigious institution requires a high level of education and intelligence. Not many people can also claim to have parents who attended elite schools in the past. 
4)Does the rate of admittance increase with income?

raw_comment:
I noticed that whether a person is a legacy or not makes a huge difference for upper middle class people. It is the hardest for this group to get in without legacy. The acceptance chance for the super wealthy is much higher regardless of legacy or not, though significantly higher with legacy.

I wonder why politicians are so concerned with the “unfair advantage“ that affirmative action gave when legacy acceptances are conveniently overlooked. “Shouldn’t we reward people based on their hard work and not luck of the draw?” A familiar argument used this past summer when affirmative action was overturned. But is being a person of color “luck of the draw”? Or is being a legacy, the majority of which are white, wealthy individuals not upholding systemic inequality?

I know a lot of legacy students and a lot of students who have been admitted into top 20s/Ivies on merit. Most of the legacy students I know are white, privileged, and relatively less accomplished than those who aren’t. In no way am I saying that legacy students are not as smart or as talented, but there is a clear favor towards these students.

“Lucky in Legacy: An Ivy’s four leaf clover”

raw_comment: This graph is highly relatable to my experience right now as a high school senior. It shows the advantage that legacy students have at Ivy League schools. You can tell from the graph that having a parent attend that college raises the applicant's chances significantly, but interestingly, it most affects the rich. This is most likely because wealthy people tend to donate to their alma mater, especially when they have a child hoping to attend that school.  This shows how the college selection process has become increasingly biased and no longer based on the student and their accomplishment. Money makes the world go around, and colleges are businesses, so income is their main focus. The more wealthy can provide this, especially when their multigenerational legacies and alumni of these institutions. I would title this graph: "College Admissions: Money Talks."

It surprises me that applicants with more wealthy parents have a lower chance of acceptance. I wonder why this is a factor. I also wonder how scholarships and financial aid play a role in this. This relates to me and my community, given that me and my peers will soon be going through the college admissions process, and it is important to know what factors admissions officers value. "Legacy Leverage"

raw_comment:
Thanks to all the students who responded.   We had many terrific headlines this week.  Here are some student headlines that popped out while capturing the main idea of the graph:  “Inherited Admission to Elite Colleges” by Sky of Iowa; “College: The Legacy of a Rich Man’s World!” by Katherine of Academy of Saint Elizabeth in Morristown, N.J.; two headlines from Perth Amboy High School in N.J. – “Nepotism at Ivy, Advantages Not Even Money Could Buy!” by Santiago and “The Legacy Continues, The Gap Widens” by Emily; “"Are You Rich? Are You a Legacy? Welcome to Harvard!" by Alyson Lu of California; 
"Who's Applying: You or Your Parents?" by Texas of Pewaukee High School in Wis. and SIX headlines from Michigan:  "Data Shows Elite Colleges Who Preach ‘Equity’ Still Favor Legacies and the Upper Class" by Seth of Traverse City West Senior High School; “No Legacy, No Money, No Luck: Bias in College Admissions” by Molly and “Ivy League or Legacy League” by Cameron, both of Gull Lake High School in Richland, Mich.;  “College Admissions Skewed: The Effects of Income and Legacy” by Maxwell of Richland, Mich.; “What Are Colleges Really Looking for in an Application: Rich Legacy Parents!” by Aidan of Mich. and “Wealth and Legacy: Is This Mainly What Admissions Care About?” by Henry of Mich.

raw_comment:
I noticed that legacy applicants had more than double the advantage of being admitted into college compared to non-legacy students. I also noticed that up until the 99.9 percentile, the graph stays kind of flat, but then it suddenly grows for both legacy and non-legacy students. The 99.9 percentile of legacy applicants have more than 6x the advantage compared to the average. Still, the 99.9 percentile of non-legacy applicants has around the same advantage or less than the poorer legacy applicants. 

I wonder why they value legacy students more than even the rich non-legacy students. I also wonder how different the admission process is for non-legacy, legacy, rich, and poor. 

It relates to me because a lot of people think that whether you go to college or not is a very important determinant of your future. As someone who wants to go to college, seeing these kinds of statistics can be discouraging, as you know someone else will be considered first in line matter what you do, especially if you are not a legacy applicant. 
“Elite Colleges Have Preferences”

raw_comment:
"There ARE Advantages in Elite College Admissions!"

Students whose parents' incomes are in the top 5% are far more likely to be admitted into a prestigious university.  Interestingly, students whose parents are in the 70-95th percentile get accepted less frequently.  Additionally, legacy applicants are far more likely to be admitted.  I wonder why universities care so much about the students' wealth in the admission process.  This means that students from low-income households and/or are first-generation college students are at a disadvantage.  Some universities are becoming need-blind and offering resources for first-generation college students to excel after high school.

raw_comment: The graph depicts how being a legacy affects an applicant's chances of being accepted into a school. For more elite schools, the familial ties a student has to a school could be the largest factor in their application, boosting their chances about 7x. Nearly all students at the most prestigious universities are legacy students. This affects me because I am in the college admissions process. My chances of being admitted to an Ivy League are very low because I am not a legacy at any of them. I wonder why this kind of bias is still allowed in the decision process. There have been legislations passed in the last few years that have taken race, economic, and standardized testing bias out of the process. The fact that legacy consideration is still used seems unethical. A possible headline could be "How are your chances of getting into Harvard?"

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
What do you notice? A: I noticed that if you have a lot of money or a legacy boost, then you are more likely to get into that college. But, if you don't have a lot of money or none of your relatives had been in that college, you're a lot less likely to get into that school.
What do you wonder? A: I wonder if those people with good money bribe or give a lot of money to these schools so that their children will get into a good school. Or if you have a higher income you will have a higher chance of getting in. 
How does this relate to you and your community? A: I'm sure some people here want to get into these schools but not a lot of us have good money, and I would say that I am in the middle class. It would be harder for me or someone else to get into that school without a lot of money. 
Elite Colleges, and Advantage Legacys

raw_comment:
This graph compares students with the same test scores and how their chances of being admitted to elite colleges can fluctuate based on their legacy status and their parent's income rank. It is clear from this graph that in every income rank, legacy status helps boost admission. I find it insane that very wealthy people with legacy status are 7x more likely to get into a school than a poorer person without status who worked just as hard to get the same scores. I wonder why there is such a bias in college admissions. One reason for this bias is that wealthy families may donate a lot of money to the school which could put applicants at an advantage. However, one would assume that by having all this wealth, one already has an advantage in life. Therefore, giving a wealthy and a poor person who both have legacy status and have the same scores unequal opportunities is solidifying those societal norms. Also the poor students not getting into these elite colleges because they are put at an inherent disadvantage when applying, it is further hindering their opportunities in life. I wonder if giving out financial aid is one of the reasons that poorer students who have the same credentials as wealthy students have a less likely chance of getting into a school. I know that financial aid is limited. This affects me because I will be applying to colleges soon. Some schools are doing away with legacy status, so I am not sure how much this graph will affect me. 
"Legacy Boost= Bias"

raw_comment:
I notice that this graph shows how much of an advantage wealthy legacy children have for getting into the country's top "prestigious" colleges. I notice that your parent's income has a big pull on your chances of acceptance. I wonder if schools will start to defer or reject more legacy to make it more fair for other applicants who are more deserving. This relates to me and my community because many of my friends including myself our top schools were schools our siblings or parents had attended. I have grown very familiar with this process over the past few months as a senior in high school. I have started to hear of a lot more legacies getting deferred in the early action applicant pool and being moved to regular decisions. 
Headline:
Is the college application process as fair as we think??

1. I notice that being in the 80th percentile of income without legacy reduces the chances of being
accepted. In contrast, students in the 99th percentile without legacy are still twice as likely to get accepted
into the school. The only time the grey dips below the average is between the 75th and 90th percentile of
earnings.
2. I wonder why students without legacy who live in families with very high incomes have greater
chances of getting into colleges, could it be other factors such as reputation?
3. Many people in my community who apply to college must compete with other students who get a
massive boost simply by being the child of a former student. This is a major struggle for people working
very hard trying to achieve their dreams only to lose to the college system.
4. "Legacy and Luxury: The Admissions Advantage Unveiled in Elite Colleges"

raw_comment: In this graph, it is measuring the relation between parents’ income, legacy status, and admissions status at the collegiate level. I noticed that, generally, richer applicants had a greater estimated legacy boost than those who were poorer. For example, as the parents’ income rank became closer and closer to 100th, the estimated legacy boost became closer and closer to 7x. Whereas, on the other hand, poorer applicants closer to an income rank of 10th seemed to only have an estimated legacy boost of 2x. I wonder if the legacy boost is greater for applicants whose parents have a higher income rank because having a richer background increases the chances of multiple generations of family members being able to afford attending that college. Additionally, I noticed that the estimated advantage in admissions for those without legacy status on the richer end of the spectrum was lower than for those who were poorer and wonder why this is the case. I also wonder what criteria and processes a school uses to determine their legacy boost discrepancies and if this varies from college to college. This applies to my community because I recently went through the college process and the concept of legacy advantage has been a concern for many. It can be unfair as applicants can have very similar profiles to legacy applicants yet those with legacy are favored over them. My headline for this graph would be: “Is Bias Overcoming the College Admissions Process?”.

raw_comment:
I noticed that legacy applicants have a significant advantage in the college admissions process over people applying without a legacy status to elite colleges. However, I also noticed that it is 7 times more likely that a legacy applicant to an elite college would be admitted if their parents’ income is higher than the 90th percentile. Additionally, I find it interesting that there is a dip in the graph for applicants without a legacy status in which the parents are around the 70th percentile in income. This is intriguing because parents with a relatively low income (in the “poorer” income rank) were two times as likely to be admitted. With this, I noticed and wondered why the “poorer” and “richer” extreme income rank almost reached the same likelihood of admittance for non-legacy applicants. Furthermore, I wonder how the graph would appear individually for each of the elite colleges (which elite college favors more legacies overall?). The graph relates to my community because as a senior, I along with many of my classmates are going through the college admissions process. This makes me wonder how money and legacies will affect my classmates’ admissions to certain colleges. 

“Are Elite Colleges Truly for Elite Students or the Elite?”

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
1. I noticed that legacy applicants had a higher chance of getting accepted. I also noticed that the rate stayed consistent almost all the way until the 50-ish mark.
2. I wonder why there is a dip in the estimated legacy boost between the 75th - 90th percentile. I also wonder why the rates boosted up so suddenly after the 50th percentile.
3. This is relevant to me and my community because soon we will be looking to apply at colleges. Will this impact what schools we consider applying to just because we are scared of rejection?
4. Are College Admissions Considering Wealth Over Grades and Work Ethic?

raw_comment:
I notice that there is a legacy boost in elite colleges, meaning that if a parent or sibling goes there you have a much higher chance of getting into that college. I noticed that students who are wealthier, have a greater chance of getting admitted as well. 
I wonder why having a parent or sibling that previously went to that college makes a effect on whether or not a student gets admitted or not, because shouldn’t the college be focusing on you, not others? 
This relates to me and my community, because I am currently a senior making college decisions, and my chances may be lower or higher than other people just based on a family legacy. 
What’s going on in this graph is that it is showing the effects of how legacy and wealth contribute to one’s chance of being admitted into college. A catchy headline would be: If You Have A Legacy, it is Your Legacy To Come To College!

raw_comment:
1) The acceptance rate for legacy applicants is higher than applicants without legacy status, especially for the top 0.1% who are 7x more likely to be accepted. 
2) Why does the acceptance rate for applicants without legacy status drop below the average? Why is the boost for the top 0.1% so high? 
3) As a high school student who will be the first in my family to attend college, I find it interesting to see other applicants' advantages over me. Even though the graph compares students with the same test scores, students who come from wealthy families have the upper hand over everyone else. 
4) Your Dream College Wants Daddy's Money, Not You

raw_comment: I notice that students with legacy status and whose parents’ income rank is in the top .1% are 7 times as likely to be admitted to a given school compared to a non-legacy student who is not incredibly wealthy. I also notice that people without legacy status who are incredibly wealthy have a much greater advantage than non-legacy students who have lower incomes. I wonder why these elite colleges place so much emphasis on financial status. If these families are already wealthy why should they be given even more advantages in life than they already have? Why do these schools not attempt to give opportunities to people who may not have a lot of money but have the potential to be incredibly successful if they are given the opportunity to study at a prestigious university? It is very likely that these legacy families donate large sums of money to the universities and continue to do so as their children and their grandchildren get accepted. These donations provide the university with significant benefits and could be a very likely reason why they choose to admit legacy students who are very likely to attend and who have parents who will provide continuous donations to the university during and after their time there. If they choose not to accept a legacy student they may lose some of their best donors, and prestigious universities make incredible amounts of money from their donations and would not want to lose these funds. -unifished see other post

Prestigious universities would not want to lose these funds.

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that legacy applications were admitted at a considerably higher rate than non-legacy applicants, irrespective of income. Additionally, the chance of admission for students with the greatest income is little over two times, which is insignificant when compared to legacy students who have a parent income rate of 99.9.
2. I wonder about the role that money plays in the school's admissions process. Is there evidence of favoritism in the amount of legacy students being admitted? What distinguishes legacy students from non-legacy students as superior applicants? Does the admission of legacy students depend on the actions of their alumni at these schools?
3. This is relevant to me and my community because I'm from an urban area, which means that many of my peers and classmates have not had the same luck as me in being legacy students or having parents who were alumni. As a result, admission is even more difficult for those who are already at a disadvantage due to circumstances beyond their control.
4. Rags To Riches, Is Favoritism Ending Fair Chances?

### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment:
I noticed that legacy students of all income rankings tend to see significantly higher admission rates than non legacy students. To a certain extent, income also seems to be a determining factor in admission rates. Students with family incomes ranking in the top 10% are much more likely to be admitted than those in the bottom 10%. 

The fact that students are more likely to be admitted when their family income is higher is interesting. Many colleges claim to be need-blind when looking at college applications, so the data has to be due to other factors possibly influenced by higher income. Students with more resources have easier access to information, tutors, or other outside assistance. These resources are likely to positively impact their academic performance and college applications. However, students with less resources end up disadvantaged in comparison to these students.

This graph sheds light on how a student’s family background — something they have no control over — can impact their lives. A student whose family has a higher income and more access to resources will typically end up more successful than one with a lower income. As a community, students with less access to resources needed for academic excellence need to be addressed. If we can address this issue, we can reduce the disparity between legacy and non-legacy, or high-income and low-income.

raw_comment:
1) I've seen that wealthier students are more likely to get into college. I've also noticed that students with legacies have a far better chance of getting accepted. It's not morally right but a common trend shown in this graph.

2) I'm curious if you can still get in on Legacy despite not being wealthy, or if it will reduce your chances. Do universities allow more wealthy students because they can repay their tuition faster than those who take out loans? I wonder if someone with superior grades or a more remarkable resume can overcome a  family legacy.

3) This affects me and my community since I know several students who will be the first in their families to attend college. My town isn't exactly wealthy, so how will they achieve their goal if legacies are preferred? My community will have a harder time if rates continue to rise at the same rate as the expected legacy increase. 

4) Did you get accepted into college or are you just a legacy?

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that legacy applications were admitted at a considerably higher rate than non-legacy applicants, irrespective of income. Additionally, the chance of admission for students with the greatest income is little over two times, which is insignificant when compared to legacy students who have a parent income rate of 99.9.
2. I wonder about the role that money plays in the school's admissions process. Is there evidence of favoritism in the amount of legacy students being admitted? What distinguishes legacy students from non-legacy students as superior applicants? Does the admission of legacy students depend on the actions of their alumni at these schools?
3. This is relevant to me and my community because I'm from an urban area, which means that many of my peers and classmates have not had the same luck as me in being legacy students or having parents who were alumni. As a result, admission is even more difficult for those who are already at a disadvantage due to circumstances beyond their control.
4. Rags To Riches, Is Favoritism Ending Fair Chances?

raw_comment:
This graph depicts the likelihood of admissions advantages at some colleges compared to those with the same test scores and a legacy advantage. I noticed how a legacy student with wealthier parents who are in the top 0.1% of income would be 7x more likely to be admitted into a school compared to a less wealthy student. That definitely must annoy students trying to get into an elite college. I also noticed that regardless of income, legacy applicants were admitted significantly higher than those without legacy status. I noticed that students who have a legacy advantage are more wealthy compared to those without a legacy advantage. I wonder how this could be and why there is a lesser legacy for students with low income. Maybe it could be because the wealthier applicants can afford high tuition costs and donate money to the elite college whereas the low-income ones would struggle and would request financial aid.  I wonder how income and legacy advantages affect a student getting into an elite college in the admission process. Like if you request financial aid are you more likely to be deferred or rejected? This affects me and my community because I am a sophomore who is starting to research college so next year I will be applying and looking at schools. Also, I have 2 older sisters who have applied to colleges without a legacy advantage and got in. But my family friend with a legacy advantage for Villanova as her uncle went there, got differed. 
Title of graph: Legacy

raw_comment:
The graph depicts the admissions rate for students with the same test scores, and how they vary based on legacy and parent's income. Legacy applicants are much more likely to be admitted. For income, the general trend seems to be that the more an applicant's parents make, the better chance they have. 
However, there is one anomaly that I noticed. For those who are not legacies, the admittance rate goes down before going back up at around the 90th percentile. I'm not sure why this is. Maybe universities try to accept students who are either extremely wealthy or poor. 
As someone who is looking to go to college, I don't want to be impaired by something I can't control, such as how much money my parents make. This inequality affects every students striving to get a higher education by promoting those who already have an advantage.
Headline: COLLEGE ADMISSIONS ASSESS MORE THAN GRADES

I noticed this anomaly.
For those who are not legacies, the admittance rate goes down before going back up at around the 90th percentile.
I am not sure why this is.
Maybe universities try to accept students who are either extremely wealthy or poor.
I am looking to go to college.
I do not want to be impaired by something that I cannot control.
How much money my parents make is something that I cannot control.
This inequality affects every students striving to get a higher education.
This inequality promotes those who already have an advantage.
The headline is: COLLEGE ADMISSIONS ASSESS MORE THAN GRADES.

raw_comment:
The graph shows the likelihood of legacy applicants to be admitted into elite colleges, in comparison to those with the same qualifications without legacy advantage. In the graph we can see a student in the top one percent is over 7 times more likely to be accepted to a school. Additionally students from wealthy parents are most likely to have parents who are legacy. I wonder how colleges are still able to accept students based on their finical status rather than Merit. I wonder with the overturning of affirmative action if this will be the next thing to go in the college admission process. This graph impacts my community because I am a student applying to college and it shows how people my own age may have a greater chance of getting into called solely based on wealth and legacy as compared to me a first generation student. 

 “ The corruption among the elites”

raw_comment:
1. What do you notice? If your notice makes a claim, where in the graph is the evidence to support it?
The legacy boost isn’t even as you move across the line of the income status (the boost for 70th is much higher than 10th, but a lot lower than 99th) 
For those in the top 10% of wealth, if they are a legacy, their chances of being accepted skyrocket, especially compared to those who aren’t legacy and are in the bottom 70-80%. 

2. What do you wonder?
What test was used?
What were the scores? 
Why did they choose only these schools? 
Did they account for the fact that some schools only removed their legacy policy in the later dates of the range of which they studied, which would skew the data? 
It’s highly unlikely that test scores are the only thing that influences admission, so what other variables could be impacting admission, such as their essay, resume, etc.? 

3. What impact does this have on you and your community?
Since I am a senior and I am applying to college, for elite colleges, I am often competing with applicants who have legacy. This makes it harder for me to get into the colleges that I want because those with legacy have a higher chance of getting in, which then lowers my own chance of acceptance. 

4. What's going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph's main idea.
Elitism of Elite Schools
Daddy's Money gets you in the Ivy League

raw_comment:
I notice that the estimated legacy boost is much higher for the top 0.1%-- they are 7 times as likely to be admitted. I also notice that the upper middle class (75-90th income rank) has a lower than average admission rate. The admissions rate tends to be higher than average for poorer and richer families.

I wonder when admission rates became so much higher for legacy students; did the trend start when admissions became more selective? I also wonder why the upper middle class is disadvantaged in college admissions. Is there anything being done to mitigate the legacy boost? Also, do the top  0.1% also fare better because they have more money to invest in extracurriculars and tutoring?

This relates to me and my community because I will enter college soon. Since my classmates and I will be competing against legacy students who have a much higher likelihood of getting into selective schools, this may hurt our chances at admissions.

This graph shows the disparate admission rates at elite colleges for legacy and nonlegacy students, as well as between the rich and the poor.

"The Golden Touch: College Admissions"

### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment: The graph depicts the likelihood of legacy applicants to be admitted into elite colleges, in comparison to those with the same test scores and no legacy advantage. As the parents’ income increases, so does the estimated legacy boost. For example, a legacy student with parents who rank in the top 0.1% of income is 7x more likely to be admitted into a school. I wonder why there is less of a legacy boost for students with parents below the 50th percentile of income rank. Perhaps the ability of the wealthier to donate a greater amount of money impacts the graph. The effect of financial aid could be another possible explanation for this. This graph impacts my community because my peers and I recently applied to college. It shows us that if we have similar academic statistics to another individual, but lack a legacy status, we are at a disadvantage for admissions. I would title this graph: “The Legacy Advantage.”

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that regardless of income, legacy applicants were admitted significantly higher than those without legacy status. Furthermore, even with the highest income the likelihood of students being admitted is slightly over 2x which pales in comparison to legacy students with a 99.9 parent income rate.
2. I wonder how income factors into the school's decisions as to who is admitted and who isn't. Does the number of legacy students being admitted suggest nepotism? What makes legacy students better applicants than non-legacy students? Does it matter what the alumni did at these schools for the legacy students to get admitted?
3. This relates to me and my community as being from an urban community it means that a number of my classmates/peers have not had the same fortune of being legacy students/having parents that were alumni thus making admission that much harder for those already at a disadvantage for something completely out of their control (with parental/guardian income also being completely out of these students control). 
4. Nepotism at Ivy, Advantages Not Even Money Could Buy!

raw_comment:
This graph shows the relationship between elite colleges and their tendency to choose applicants based on economic status and family legacy. The black line (legacy applicants) is significantly higher than the gray line (non-legacy applicants), indicating that elite schools favor legacy. Legacy applicants appear to automatically have double the chance of acceptance. It also is apparent that both types of applicants have a spiked chance of getting into an elite college when their parent’s income surpasses the 90th percentile. One thing that stood out to me was that applicants without legacy status who fall into the middle-class range have less of a chance of getting into an elite college versus an applicant with poorer or richer incomes. Do colleges discriminate against middle-class applicants because they are not rich enough to generate a lot of money but not poor enough to even out their statistics in appearing to be a school that caters to all financial statuses? This relates to me because as a current senior applying to college, I have had to witness students get into colleges just because of their legacy status and or because their parents have a higher income. Even though we may have the same statistics, colleges will always choose the legacy/richer applicant. This has hurt all students, not just me, and our disadvantage when it comes to being a middle class without legacy status when applying to elite colleges. 

“College: The Legacy of a Rich Man’s World!”

A person can have a relative.

A person can apply to the college.
A person can have parents.
The parents can have income.
Having a relative that attended the college relates to the chances of being admitted.
The parents’ income relates to the chances of being admitted.
From 9 am to 2 pm E.T. today, you can share responses online.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
1) I noticed that having a higher amount of income will still cost your chances of being admitted itself, but legacy does make a huge difference.

2) I wonder if there are other factors that play a huge role in college admission, apart from test scores and grades.

3) This relates to me because it gives me an idea of how I might stack up against other applicants of different demographics.   

4) "What Colleges Actually Look For"

raw_comment:
1. I notice the extreme boost in odds of admission for legacy applicants and for some reason a boost for kids with parts in the top 1-5%. I also notice there isn’t my change based on income at any point below the top 5%. Finally I notice that strangely income has a much greater effect for legacy applicants than it does for normal applicants. 
2. I wonder why there is such a difference between normal and legacy applicants as you would expect the college to accept the best applicants and not those with parents who went there previously. I also wonder how income can affect admissions so greatly, I wonder if it is because they have more support to build a better application or if it's actually part of the admissions calculations. Finally I wonder why income hurts your chances as a normal applicant (until top 5ish percent) though it boosts your chances as a legacy applicant. 
3. Admission to college is very important to me and to many other students in my community. The majority of us are not legacy applicants and if there is truly this great disadvantage that comes with this fact it would greatly damage the hopes of many students in my district. Additionally my district is very split on parents' financial status and truly this shouldn’t be affecting admissions as I don’t think the most supported kids are very often the smartest or hardest working students. 
4. College Admissions Skewed: The Effects of Income and Legacy

1) I noticed the likelihood of being admitted into a prestigious institution increases with your income. Compared to someone who is regarded as poor, their odds are about four times higher. 

2) I wonder if richer applicants typically get admitted to colleges because they can pay for their tuition more quickly than those who take out loans? Are they considered more reliable?

3) This relates to me and my community because a lot of my friends and I are applying to colleges and there are more factors that go in to being accepted than we would have thought. Many of us would have to take out a loan and this may affect our acceptance.

4) Money over Intelligence When it comes to College Acceptance?

1. I notice that based on the graph the higher income a family has the higher the likelihood is of getting accepted into college. Meanwhile the lower the income a family may have the likelihood of getting accepted into college is lower. As well as any applicants with legacy's have up to 7 times the chance of getting accepted into college. While applicants without a legacy only are 2 times more chance of getting accepted into college.

2. I wonder why colleges have a "favorite" with the higher income applicants, why should they be highly accepted while the low income applicants don't? At the end of the day both of the applicants are going to end up paying a similar amount of money at college. 3. This relates to me and my community because for many people  in my town which include my family, we normally come from different countries and due to such reason  some families may not have a high income compared to others. raw_comment:
1. What I notice is that the higher the student's parent income is, the more likely they are to get accepted to college, while people who have a lower income are less likely to be accepted to college. I also recognize how leacy helps students get accepted, as for those who do not have it, they get under the average admission rate.
2. I wonder how far does having a higher income take you. For instance, if you are rich but you do not meet the college requirements, will they still accept you for your income as if you have a lower income but you meet all the requirements?
3. This is relevant to my community because it contributes to social inequality by limiting educational opportunities for those who have a lower income. It's not fair that just because you have a higher income, you mostly get accepted to college compared to someone who struggles financially. This creates an environment where success is tied to a higher income.
4. "The Secret to Instant Admission Revealed!"

raw_comment:
It appears that as Parents’ income rank rises, so does the estimated legacy boost. Also, up until roughly the 85th percentile for Parents’ income rank, there is a decrease in the likelihood to be admitted, whereas there is on average a consistent likelihood to be admitted after the legacy boost. Finally, after the 85th percentile, there is an increase in admission likelihood for both students with and without the legacy boost, although there is a more drastic increase for those with the legacy boost.
I wonder why the likelihood of admittance for legacy applicants remains close to constant for most of the graph. Why does the likelihood of admittance drop so drastically for non-legacy applicants around the 70th Parent’s income rank?
For people from my community, this shows that those with parents who graduated from selective schools are more likely to be admitted than those whose parents did not graduate from a selective school. We could also generalize this to more than just the twelve selective schools and say that applicants are more likely to be admitted to a school if their parents graduated from there. 
“Systemic generational bias in higher education”

My town isn't exactly wealthy, so how will they achieve their goal if legacies are preferred? My community will have a harder time if rates continue to rise at the same rate as the expected legacy increase. 

4) Did you get accepted into college or are you just a legacy?

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
1.Once the parents' income rank reached the 99th percentile, there was a major jump in admissions advantage. The first jump in estimated boost begins around the 70th income rank percentile for both legacy and non-legacy applicants. Around the 50th and 80th percentile advantage for students with and without legacy status goes down. 

2.I wonder what caused a drop in admission advantages for both groups of students around the 50th and 80th percentiles.

3.In my high school, every year students apply to all different kinds of colleges and universities. It is both interesting and tiresome to see that just because of a student's income and legacy status, they might have a better chance of getting into college. It is sad that students that have scores and resumes on the same level may get rejected from certain colleges and universities because of things that they cannot control.

4.Should Legacies Be the Only Ones to Leave a Legacy?

raw_comment:
What do you notice? 
-The applicants with parents on the wealthier end of the spectrum who are not legacies are less likely to get accepted then poorer non-legacy applicants. 
-People with rich, alumni parents are the most likely to be accepted into the university. 
-Where the rich alumni admissions spike, the rich non-alumni applicant admissions drop. 

What do you wonder? 
-Why are these students applying? What is their reasoning behind their applications? 

What’s the relation? 
Both of my parents are alumnis at Michigan State University. It thus follows that I’ve grown up going to campus- I love it up there, however that doesn’t mean that I want to go there. However, a lot of people want (or expect) me to go there because of my parents. I wonder then, what the pressure on the legacies is. Perhaps the Ivy Leagues favor legacies. But, could it also be that a lot of the legacies applying there apply because they are legacies? 

Title: 
A Legacy of Parental Pressure

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment:
1. What do you notice? If your notice makes a claim, where in the graph is the evidence to support it?

This graph demonstrates the likelihood of acceptance to an elitist college by comparing factors including wealth and whether the student is a legacy, all with the same test scores. It shows that being a legacy immediately gives one an advantage no matter their current social status but also that as they become wealthier their chances increase with the top 0.1% being 7 times more likely to be accepted. 

2. What do you wonder?

I wonder why there are a few dips in the graph, especially right around the 0.5% middle class and the people right before the top 0.1%. What makes them less likely to be accepted?

3. What impact does this have on you and your community?

This impacts me and my community because me and my classmates are currently applying to college. It's important that we understand the hidden factors that inflounce our acceptance apart from our test scores. 

4. What's going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph's main idea.

Is being a legacy no longer a promise of acceptance? 

Top 0.1% fueling their own success, is being wealthy the real deciding factor in college acceptances?

raw_comment:
1. According to the graph, I notice that legacy applicants with a wealthier background overall have a greater opportunity of being admitted into elite colleges. Whereas those with no legacy and a poorer status have a significantly lower possibility of being admitted into these elite colleges. In the graph, as parents' income rank continues to increase, applicants with a legacy can be up to 7x more likely to be admitted to elite colleges compared to those who have no benefits and no legacy which only fall into a little more than 2x probability of being admitted.
2. Even though all students had the same test scores, I wonder why colleges choose to accept those with an overall financially stable background rather than giving an equal opportunity to all students. Is this due to colleges believing that wealthier students will be able to pay off any college debt compared to poorer students?
3. This relates to me and my community because being that high schoolers are preparing to take on important decisions when choosing their dream colleges, these aspects that colleges are looking at can affect the acceptance rate of their admissions being accepted. 
4. Step 1:Money, Step 2:A legacy, Great, Welcome to college!

raw_comment: I notice that this graph, very clearly shows that, despite colleges claiming it doesn't matter, being a legacy applicant as well as being in the top 1% of income dramatically increases the chances of being admitted to elite colleges.  The evidence for this is clearly shown on the furthest right end of the graph when it increases exponentially starting at around 90th percentile or top 10%.  This is even more dramatic when those people are legacy applicants.    I wonder if colleges will ever truly not take legacy into account and give everyone equal opportunities regardless of whether their family attended the school.  This relates to me and my community because this means we have less chance of getting into those kinds of high-end colleges compared to someone who is a legacy and also extremely rich.  A catchy headline could be "Data Shows Elite Colleges Who Preach "Equity" Still Favor Legacies and the Upper Class."

raw_comment:
I notice that legacy students have a higher admissions rate than non legacy students no matter how wealthy they are. Whether you are legacy or not, your wealth provides you a significant advantage in getting in. Legacy students who are also wealthy see an even more significant boost in admission likelihood, around 7x than the average. I wonder if colleges had less monetary incentive  if the admissions rate for legacy students would be lower. Both of these factors can prevent average people who don't have historically wealthy families from climbing climbing the income ladder. 

Is legacy privilege unfair to average families?

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
