raw_comment:
I notice that the wealthier you and your family are, the higher chance you have of getting into an Ivy League college. This is shown in the graph when the top 0.1% is 7x more likely to get into an Ivy League college compared to others.

I wonder whether Ivy League colleges are more focused on the wealth and status of a student rather than their academic successes and abilities. It seems like these schools prefer and favor the richer and wealthier students so that makes me question if these schools aren't simply for the smartest, but for the wealthiest.

This relates to me and my community because many people in my community dream of attending an Ivy League school. But since they don't have the status that these schools favor, their chances are very low and require nothing short of a miracle to even have a chance to attend these schools.

Is Your Social Status Worthy Enough For the Ivy Leagues?

raw_comment:
I notice that when it comes to comparing students with the same test scores and figuring out who to admit to an elite college, legacy applicants have a much better chance of getting admitted. But, when money is added to the picture, non-legacy applicants that come from poorer families have a higher chance of being admitted than wealthy non-legacy applicants.This trend is shown throughout with the top 0.1% being 7x more likely to get in than someone with comparing attributes but no legacy.

I wonder why the colleges pay attention to the students' background and income more than their test scores. If a non-legacy student had a higher test score than a legacy student would it convince the colleges to admit them over the legacy student?

This relates to me and my community because as a high schooler, we are preparing to enter college and pursue a career meaning people my age or older have to take in consideration of these details, we must be aware of our possibilities and take advantage of what we can.

raw_comment:
1) Without legacy status, I've seen that your chances of getting admitted are lower the more money your parents make, unless you are among the top 0.1%.  However, students with legacy status do not fully follow the trend.  The admittance rate for legacy status students often rises with the income of their parents; if you are among the top 0.1%, your chances are nearly doubled.
2)I wonder why the admissions process to Ivy League colleges is so competitive; the likelihood of being accepted increases dramatically with income. I believe this is the case because elite universities want wealthy students, and wealthy parents will undoubtedly pay them a lot of cash to get their inferior kids into such esteemed institutions. I believe that institutions have the conceited right to raise tuition and elevate their status when so-called "elites" enroll in Ivy League schools.
3)This is relevant to my town as a large number of individuals there are preparing to enter college, and if this is taken into account when making admissions decisions, it might lead to issues with college admittance.
4) "Dear College Am I Rich Enough For You"

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
1)
I notice that there's about a 2x boost for legacies of any kind, and that it stays constantly above the non-legacy line. I also notice that the extremely wealthy legacies, ie; the 0.1 percentile are nearly 7x more likely to be accepted. Considering that elite colleges have an acceptance rate of about 3.3% to 7.3%, the acceptance rate for rich legacies is about 23.1% to 51.1%. This is important because traditionally, people who go to elite colleges are more likely to be rich in the future. The boost given to wealthy people and to legacies is meant to keep the class divide obvious and the school selective. 
 
2)
I wonder if this legacy boost exists in every single college. What would happen if each college had an individual graph plotting the difference between legacy and non-legacy? Does this bias go on to affect other things at the college, like grades, clubs, etc? Why is there a dip in the graph at around 50th and below the 90th?  What is it about these top 0.1 percentile legacies that makes colleges so incredibly drawn to them?

3)
This relates to my and my community because I am planning to go to college one day, and being a middle class non-legacy, it feels discouraging to apply to Ivy League and other elite schools. And even if I were to get accepted into these schools, many of the people I would be attending with would also be extremely wealthy, leading to a difference in experience and in values. 

4)
"Are You Rich? Are You a Legacy? Welcome to Harvard!"

raw_comment:
I notice that according to the graph the children who come from the top 0.1% of wealth in America are 7 times more likely to be admitted in elite colleges.  I wonder if the mode for the poor population of students are targeted by colleges or if its just by chance. For the poor population the 30th percentile mark has the highest chance to be admitted to a college. The chance proceeds to decline before and after that percentile only ascending again when it goes to the 70th percentile. This relates to me and my community because many people in Perth Amboy come from the lower financial side. It is very discouraging to realize that even though my peers and I are very educated and intelligent our application to elite colleges are looked differently than of an application that comes from wealth. 
Colleges Care About your $$$$

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
The very first thing I noticed while looking at the graph is how much more likely legacy students are to be admitted into college. There is a significant trend indicating how legacy students are far more likely to be admitted into ivy league colleges. The second thing I noticed is how rich-non legacy kids are less likely to be admitted compared to poor-non legacy students. The third thing I notice is how the percentage of rich-legacy students have the biggest advantage in getting into the Ivy League.

What do you wonder?
I wonder why the admission officials care so much about legacy. Does it increase profit? I also wonder why more wealthy kids who have no legacy have less of a chance of being admitted into college. 

How does this affect me and my community?
My classmates and I are going to be starting the college process very shortly and knowing the chances with legacy and whatnot can be a huge factor. It makes many students ask the question- “do I have less of a chance of getting into a college because I have no legacy and no wealth”? It seems to create an unfair advantage to me and my community which is very concerning to some.

Headline
Wealth and legacy- is this mainly what admissions care about?

raw_comment:
1) I notice that legacy students have a greater chance overall of being admitted into elite schools than students without the status. After the 50th percentile, while the chances to get admitted for legacy students went up, it had gone down for students without the status. Students without the status chances of being admitted had decreased the richer their families were, even going below average until after the 90th percentile of parents' income rank as their chances rose again. 

2) Why is the legacy status valued more? Why did the chance of being admitted decrease so much for normal students between the 70th and after the 90th percentile of parents' income? How many students hold the legacy status in these schools?

3) As many people are applying to college, it seems rather unfair that they'll have fewer chances of being admitted if they don't have the legacy status. As applying to elite schools is already very competitive, it is a great disadvantage as normal students also do the hard work getting the same grades, yet have less chances of getting in. When in all ways, students with legacy status always have the benefit of the doubt if they'll get in or not.

4) The College Admission Game: Perks of Being Legacy Status

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
		I notice that without legacy status the more money your parents make the less of a chance that you will get admitted unless you are in the top 0.1%.  The trend does not completely carry over to legacy status students though.  Legacy status students acceptance rate generally increases the more their parents make and if you are in the top 0.1%, then your chances are about doubled.
What do you wonder?
	I wonder how long this trend will continue.  I also wonder which colleges they got these statistics from.  
How does this relate to you and your community?
	I am a high school junior, so I wonder how this will impact me and my peers when we apply to colleges.
Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
	Parents make or break college admissions

raw_comment:
I notice that there is an obvious correlation between parents’ income and the likelihood of getting into an Ivy League. Also, when your parents or another close relative went to that college, the chances of getting accepted are about doubled for every income level. Additionally, I notice a subtle increase in acceptance rates at the low end of the income spectrum, presumably because of financial aid.

I wonder why the Ivy League college system is like this; your chances of getting in are significantly larger if you’re extremely wealthy. I think it’s because the colleges like to have rich people attending their schools and the rich surely pay them a large amount of under-the-table money to get their sub-par children into such renowned schools. I think when so-called “elites” attend the Ivy Leagues, colleges then have the self-adorned right to raise tuition and make their school more prestigious.

This relates to me because I am a high school junior looking to go to a good college and I am curious about acceptance rates vs. income and other factors, such as the legacy status. My entire community is mostly just my school and a small town, so I suppose many of the people in my community (at least those whom I regularly interact with) would be concerned about similar issues within the elite college community.

Catchy headline: Ive League Colleges: Wealthy in Knowledge or Wealthy in Reputation? Discover the astonishing magnitude of “elite” colleges’ lust for money and power

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
When it comes to comparing students with the same test scores and figuring out who to admit to an elite college, legacy applicants have a much better chance of getting admitted. But, when money is added to the picture, non-legacy applicants that come from poorer families have a higher chance of being admitted than wealthy non-legacy applicants.
What do you wonder?
I wonder why the colleges pay attention to the students' background and income more than their test scores. If a non-legacy student had a higher test score than a legacy student would it convince the colleges to admit them over the legacy student?
How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to me because I am currently in high school and I will start preparing for college some time next year, and if I'm not a legacy student, or a poor non-legacy student, but I still get good test scores, will I have a good chance of getting admitted?

raw_comment:
I noticed that as your parent's income rank increases, the better chance you have at getting accepted. I also noticed that the legacy applicants have more than double the shot at getting in, rather than the non-legacy students. Taking another look at the 90th rank and beyond, we see that there is a dip in acceptance and then a 7x increase. I know that people at either end of the graph have greater chances of attending because of diversity applicants and federal grant applicants, while juxtaposing that idea, the elite rich love to seperate themselves from others so the right side of the graph is not unexpected. 
I wonder if other colleges at local and community levels would have similar looking graphs? What about on the international level? I wonder if we did this survey again in 10 years would we yield the same results? 
This impacts my community because my friends and I are not legacies and yet some of them try so much harder, it's hard to know that students would be chosen over them just based on famialial history. 
"Colleges are the new alabama, Keeping it in the family"

raw_comment:
We notice that students who are legacy applicants of elite colleges have a significantly higher chance of being admitted into the college than those without legacy status. We also notice that applicants are more likely to be admitted if their parents' income rank is among the highest overall.

We wonder why is the Estimated Legacy Boost much higher between richer people who are legacy applicants and who aren't versus between poorer people who are legacy applicants and who aren't?

This relates to our community because in our high school, majority of the students come from middle-class income families and could have a chance at being accepted into elite colleges. The distribution of admission rates shows that elite colleges have hidden preferences over legacy students versus non legacy students, as well as richer families being more accepted than poorer families. Since we come from middle-class families, we should be more unsure about our chances of admission if we apply to an elite college, especially that we may not have a legacy boost to.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment:
I noticed that elite colleges pay attention to a whole lot more than your grades and test scores. They look at how rich your parents are or how poor. Also, they look at if they have gone to their school or any elite school before. Depending on those results you may be more or less likely to be accepted. 

I wonder why they base their decisions based off of parental income and where their parents went. What does that do with the current kid? Are we really just going to trash his application based off of a background he or she has absolutely no control over?

This relates heavily to me because I am a student with grades applicable to elite colleges and schools and knowing that they are going to base my acceptance based on our income amount and where my parents went to school can be a very important thing to consider. Knowing I am already at a disadvantage because of that makes me realize just how much harder I and many other students in the same situation have to work to get ahead of these other more privileged students.

Elite Schools Throwing Away Elite Applicants for Family Legacies.

raw_comment:
I noticed that overall, regardless of income, legacy applicants have a much better chance of getting into elite colleges. However, non-legacy applicants who come from poorer families have a better chance than non-legacy applicants who come from wealthier families except for the very rich. The very rich non-legacy applicants have the best chance overall out of the non-legacy applicants.
I wonder if the college looks at the students' backgrounds. Is this why the legacy applicants have a better chance? Would having higher test scores than a legacy applicant still put you at a disadvantage? How much does income really compare to legacy status?
This relates to me because I am a high school student who is going to be applying to colleges soon. I want to know how much income and legacy will really affect my chances at different colleges. This relates to my community because my friends are going to have to go through the exact same process. It will be interesting to see how the applications and results shake-up when it is all finished.
Are Colleges Picking Favorites?

raw_comment:
I noticed that there is a significant gap between the legacy boost and the estimated advantage without the legacy boost. Even without the legacy boost the graph shows that when you are in the 99.9 percentile of your parents' income rank, you are still around 3x more likely to be accepted into an IV school. 

I wonder if students ever have an issue with this and sometimes feel that they just got into these schools because of their parents. I feel like I would feel that I didn't deserve to be there and I would want to get into the school on my own. I also wonder if the parents will notice this and begin to do something about it or if they just do not care enough. 

This relates to me because I will be soon applying to colleges and it is interesting to learn more about statistics. It is interesting to learn that my community is middle-upper class and that diminishes our chances of getting into an IV league school. It is kind of frustrating to learn that income is a part of being accepted into college, while it should be about your education levels and many other things not including your parents' income.

raw_comment:
1) In general, applicants whose parents' income percentile rank is 90 or above have a significantly greater chance (up to 7 times more likely) to get accepted at an elite college. Legacy applicants are more likely to get accepted than non-legacy applicants in every income bracket.

2) I wonder why legacy boosts still exist. Why is status and income favored over ability?  

3) Many people are led to believe that college is synonymous with success. They put a lot of time and effort to meet the criterion for attending these elite schools. Yet because of their income and status, they are not even considered. Thus, the wealth inequality becomes even greater. 
 
4) The Legacy Continues, The Gap Widens

raw_comment:
Notice:
Admission spikes for the top 0.1% regardless of a student's legacy status. 
For poorer students, the admission gap between legacy and non-legacy applicants is fairly stable. For applicants above the fiftieth percentile, legacy students have a much higher advantage. 
Admittance of non-legacy students dips after the 70th percentile and spikes again around 99.
Non-legacy students have the best admittance if either above the 90th percentile or below the 50th: upper/middle-class students don't have great chances unless legacy. probably because neither in need of financial aid nor willing/able to fund programs. 
Wonder: 
Other than test scores, legacy status, and parental income, what factors are considered? How would extracurriculars, GPA, and other factors change this? - many schools no longer consider test scores (especially elite colleges). 
Relate: 
As a high school student, I am fairly anxious about college admissions. I've seen a lot of people with really impressive applications who weren't accepted into the schools they wanted. I know someone personally who graduated high school in 2022 with good grades, great test scores, and a year in an exchange program and still got in almost nowhere she applied. 
Headline: 
No Legacy, No Money, No Luck: Bias in College Admissions

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
I noticed that when applying for colleges, out of everyone with the exact same test score, legacy students are over twice as likely to be admitted. Furthermore, out of those legacy students, the children whose family has a higher income and makes more money are chosen above the others. A legacy student with a family income in the 99th percentile is 7x more likely to be admitted than the average student with the same test scores. 
I wonder why the colleges are prioritizing financial status when students apply for college. Why has our country’s education system turned away from helping educate students and form capable adults? Why is making more money a bigger priority than equality? I wonder how and if we can fix this inequality. Our country is based off of the idea of equal opportunity, and yet this is anything but equal. 
This affects me as an upcoming senior looking into colleges. It is discouraging knowing that no matter how much I study, other students will still have an unfair advantage. This affects my research in which colleges I might apply to. Knowing I might have a better chance as a legacy student is unfair, but an advantage that some might not help but to exploit. Knowing this data is frustrating, yet it is an advantage to be well informed going into college searches. 
“College Acceptance : the turn from equality and opportunity to moneymaking and legacy”

raw_comment:
I've noticed that, despite college missions departments' claims to the contrary, legacy applicants and parental income have a significant impact on applications. As the information about your parents' making expands, so does the gray region that separates the non-legacy line (bottom and grey) from the legacy line (top and black).
I'm curious as to why the graph exhibits a slight decrease at the 90th percentile of wealth. It makes sense that the admittance rate would rise with wealth, at least based on my educated prediction in the previous question. 
This is relevant to both my town and myself because I know many individuals, including myself, who are always applying to colleges, and there are several prestigious universities in the area that might be prejudiced in their selection process at the moment because of things like legacy status and financial means.
The Reality of How Rigged The College Admission Process Is

raw_comment:
1. This graph represents the likelyness of students with the same test scores but different legacy situations have of getting into an elite school. I noticed that throughout the whole graph, whether rich or poor, legacy students have a better chance of getting into an elite college. I noticed the richer they are, the higher the boost will get as well, the top .1% has 7 times the chance of getting admitted.
2. I wonder why students haven't complained about how unfair it is to get into an elite school. I also wonder just how much money a rich legacy parent must donate so that schools turn their eye and let students in. I wonder how much of a factor test scores make for admissions.
3. This affects my community as a senior as we are looking to get into colleges and have to see the competition we are getting put up against. Rich non legacy students have a lesser chance of getting into the elite schools because they dont fit the criteria of poor diverse students they want to make their school look good or rich legacy students which can throw money at them.
4.  "Is College Rigged"

raw_comment:
I notice: The top income bracket has the most likely chance of getting into elite colleges by a large margin Without legacy status, students are much less likely to get into prestigious colleges. Without a legacy boost, it seems that people in the 50-85th income percentile are less likely to get in than lower income brackets. 

I wonder: What are some reasons why the wealthier are more likely to be admitted? Is it because the colleges recognize a chance for a payday? Why is there such a legacy boost? 
Would this trend continue at some less prestigious colleges? 

Relate to me and my community: Admissions statistics for colleges are going to be very important to me as well as many in my community who are looking to go to college in the next few years and trends such as these are concerning as many do not have legacy to any prestigious colleges which diminishes chances of getting in if we choose to apply. In addition, most in my area are middle class which also diminishes our chances of getting into prestigious programs according to the graph.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
I notice the systemic barriers which keep the rich able to obtain a good education. Those systemic barriers being the legacy boost during college admissions- especially to top selective universities. This graph is appalling by the means which it keeps the less fortunate less fortunate; and, the rich richer via generational wealth and nepotism. 

What do you wonder?
I wonder why these colleges still implement legacy boosts during the college admissions process. I wonder what benefit do these universities get by preferring legacy students? Also, how does legacy boost- in addition to affirmative action- completely deplete the middle class’ chance of attending an elite university?

How does this relate to you and your community?
Residing in a rural area I and my fellow peers would most likely be unable to attend an elite university due to the systemic boost of legacy admits- which many legacies attend wealthy private schools. Additionally, we might also not have the different resources for the criterion of admissions at elite universities- which wealthy private schools might have readily available.

raw_comment:
I notice that the richer families with a legacy boost are more easily admitted to high prestige colleges whereas poorer families have to work harder to be admitted due to their financial situation.

I wonder how this graph would look in more present time and if the situation has worsened or gotten better.  I also wonder why high prestige colleges look for applicants with a legacy; instead of the applicants with the best scores and criterion.

This relates to my community because people tend to judge others based on their education and what college they attended.  I am also starting to look at colleges to attend and I find it crazy that some elite colleges are this biased towards high income and legacies.

"Attending College May Not be as Easy as it Seems"

raw_comment:
What do you notice?

The trends for legacy and standard applicants almost mirror each other, however, legacy admissions remain higher throughout the whole graph. Also, as income increases, the admission rate increases. My guess is they would like to fuel donations to the school by admitting highly wealthy individuals.

What do you wonder?

I wonder why there is a tiny dip in the graph around the 90th percentile of wealth. You would expect, at least with my guess in the last question, that the admission rate should increase for more wealth, which is generally true. 

How does this relate to you and your community?

My biggest community around me is my classmates. As we are approaching senior year, the college application process is less than a year away. While it may not contribute any useful information to my applications, it highlights the inequalities in the admissions process. Especially when considering elite colleges such as the ones polled, it alters my perspective of the identity of the university. While presenting itself as a prestigious and elite school, it looks as if all they want is your money.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment: One of the things I noticed about this graph is that without the legacy boost, in around the 70-80th income rank it actually drops, but in the same spot, it grows for legacy students. I also noticed that the x-axis consistently grows by twenty until the end where it only grows by 9.9. I think they did this to make the system seem more rigged than it is. One thing I wonder is how this has changed over time, gotten better or worse, etc. This relates to me/and my community because it shows that the college system is unfair and that will affect basically everyone who applies to colleges. I would name this: A Look Into Unfair Biases In Colleges.

raw_comment:
- I notice that legacy applicants always have a higher chance of being accepted, no matter how rich or poor. Also, for non-legacy applicants, the ranking with the second highest chance of being accepted is 30th, and the lowest is between 80 and 90, which shows that it's not a perfectly direct correlation between how rich you are and how likely you are to get into the school. However, for legacy applicants, the line is more extreme and favors richer students more.
- I wonder how this data was collected and why both lines have a tiny peak right before they get really steep. Additionally, I wonder if the problem would be solved by colleges not having access to parents' income information or whether the applicant is a legacy. The latter would be harder to accomplish, but it seems like withholding the info about money would be relatively easy.
- This relates to my community because I interact with both people who might be legacy applicants and people who might be the first in their family to go to college. The fact that these people would have different chances of getting into an elite college despite having the same test scores is truly frustrating and unfair.  
- My headline is "Affluent Legacy Applicants Receive Extreme Advantages in Elite College Admissions"

raw_comment:
1. I notice how non-legacy applicants from the 10th to 65th percent have a 2x advantage of being accepted in the universities compared to non-legacy applicants from the 65th to 90th. The graph decreases from the 65th to 90th percent income applicants, explaining how richer students are disadvantaged in college acceptance even with the same test scores. After the 90th to the 99th percent, acceptance significantly increases to approximately 2.5 times more.

For legacy applicants, the average admissions rate on the poor side begins at 2.5x. Unlike the non-legacy students, the chance of being accepted as a legacy student increases from 3x to 3.5x after 50 percent. A similar outstanding increase at the 99th percentile is also illustrated, reaching 7x the likelihood of being admitted!

2. I wonder why the two lines went in opposite directions after reaching the 50th percent of income. Why did the legacy applicants have an advantage while the non-legacy applicants had a disadvantage after 50? Have colleges made an effort to change this system of discrimination since 2015? I also wonder if the families in the 99th percentile are admitted for the funding they can offer to the school.

3. This article relates to me because when I apply to universities, being a legacy or non-legacy student for specific schools and my parents' income would significantly affect my admissions.

4. Are you disadvantaged in being accepted into some of the most prestigious universities in the United States?

Notices: I notice that as the Parent's income get's larger, the applicant's chance of getting in is higher. I also notice that kids who've had a relative attend before them are also more likely to get in.
Wonders: I wonder what colleges these include. What were these test scores? What if we compared this by race, or citizens versus not? Does coming from a 90th percentile family (income vise) give you more of an advantage versus being a legacy? How does this compare year by year?
Community: This impacts the community because if you want to get into an elite college, but you are not a legacy student or come from a poorer background, but have the same test scores of a legacy student who comes from a richer background, you may be more likely to be rejected. Also, I will be applying to colleges in a few years so these biases will matter to me!
A good headline might be: The Admissions Boost of Legacy students & richer families.

raw_comment:
Notice: I notice that legacy applicants have a greater chance of getting admitted to an elite college no matter the parents’ income. I also notice that there is a significant spike in both legacy and non-legacy applicants in the 99.9 parents’ income rank. I also notice that the difference between legacy and non-legacy applicants isn’t super significant until around the 70th rank of parents’ income. 
Wonder: I wonder why the gap between legacy and non-legacy applicants isn’t significant until after the 70th rank for parents’ income? I also wonder why the admissions rate for non-legacy applicants dips below the average admissions rate between the 70th and 90th ranks for parents’ income? I wonder why the spike is so much higher for legacy applicants than non-legacy applicants after the 90th rank for parents’ income? I wonder what test score is being examined for this graph?
Relate to You: This is applicable to me as the admissions advantage is present in all colleges, not just elite colleges. In order to get into college, I had to submit essays and complete applications regarding my parents’ education and financial status. Although I didn’t think about it at the time, I am sure that this information was probably part of the process of determining whether I would be admitted or not.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |
raw_comment:
What do you notice?
In general, rich and legacy applicants are much more likely to be accepted than poorer and/or non-legacy applicants. The acceptance rate for legacy students generally increases the wealthier the family, while the acceptance rate for non-legacy students curves down before spiking. Both types of students acceptance rates spike at the 99.9th income rank.

What do you wonder?
At around the 85th income rank, the admission likeliness for non-legacy students drops below the average admissions rate. I wonder why it drops below at that particular income rank, and not the less-wealthy applicants - could financial aid have something to do with it?

How does this relate to you and your community? 
This relates to me because I'll be applying to college pretty soon, and it's important to know the underlying discrimination of the college admissions process that only continues to grow more relevant.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?

Legacy applicants and students with higher SES parents are more likely to be admitted to elite colleges.

What do you wonder?

What moderator or mediator variables are under the hood here? SES is not revealed on an application but how does 18 years of having parents with a higher SES impact the quality of a student's application?

How does this relate to you and your community?

Mississippi residents often stay in the state for college. Therefore, few Mississippi students have parents who attended one of these elite colleges. Also, the SES of Mississippi is relatively low. Does this make it unlikely for Mississippi students to get admitted to elite colleges? What makes a college elite and why are there no elite colleges in the south? What is the return on investment (ROI) on attending an elite college? 

Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.

"Cycles of Power & Privilege"

raw_comment:
What do you notice?

I notice that those whose families are richer it is written “Estimated legacy boost” which refers to the cycle of, those with money end up with a better education and in better colleges which then boost their legacy and makes them richer once again, whereas the poorer families often don't have the same opportunities for education to those who have more money and they have to work harder because they can’t rely as much on their financial situations.

What do you wonder?

I noticed that it says that this data is from over the year 1999-2015 so, how would this graph compare to another graph of the same situation but in 2023, has this problem gotten better or worse? I wonder what benefits does it bring to a school if a kid has money or a legacy? How might this compare to a graph of non-elite colleges?, I’d assume that they might be less influenced by money considering they have less of a legacy.

How does this relate to you and your community?

In my community people use what college their family attends/ attended as a way to compare people, but this graph has shown me that if it not a fair comparison because of the obvious influence that money and relations has on prestigious schools.

Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.

How your families financial situation can influence your education opportunities.

raw_comment:
Notices:
- If your parents have a higher income and if you have a relative who went to an elite college then you have a very high chance of being accepted into that college too. 
- Legacy applicants who have parents who have lower income have about the same chance of being accepted as non-legacy applicants who have parents who have a lot higher income.

Wonders:
- What would this graph would look like if you put colleges from today on here and how would it would compare to the data from 1999 to 2015.
- Is there anything that could change about the bias towards parents with higher income of legacy status?

Relates:
- This relates to me and my community because I know people that are applying to college and it is crazy to think about how elite colleges near me might be making these biased decisions based of off the parents income and legacy status.

raw_comment:
I noticed that students with parents who have a higher income are more likely to be accepted. While the bottom 50% are about 3x as likely to be accepted, while it climbs quite tremendously in the top 50%. It escalates to the summit where the top 0.1% are 7x more likely to be admitted. I also noticed that without a legacy boost, the top 0.1% has about the same chance to get in as the bottom 10% with a legacy boost. I wonder if this happens because kids with wealthier parents have more money for better education. I also wonder if it has to do with dropping out because of mental illness which can be more common in poorer accommodations. This relates to me and my community because while I go to a school that offers scholarships and is welcoming no matter how wealthy the family is, I know many people don't live like that. Many students get bullied for how much or little money they have. This graph shows the bias in our community toward the wealthy, often because they are able to afford a better education. While people may think that that's fair, it's not really because the parents of these children likely work just as long if not longer than the top 0.1%. The difference is that the top 0.1% was able to make a money harvesting company that the lower class people may not have been able to do because of the bias in society. To sum up, we have a bias in our community because of poverty status

raw_comment:
When I look at the chart for a student with no legacy only, it looks like colleges are trying to “fix” income inequality, except perhaps at the top. A parent-income between 50% and 85% seems to work against the student, and the chance of admission without legacy is exceptionally low. 

I wonder why the legacy boost also dips around the 85% percentile and if donations to colleges increase as parents' income exceeds the 85% mark.

I also wonder what the exact student numbers of legacy vs. non-legacy are for every parent-income number. If legacy is a relatively small number of students, it may be justified for colleges to give this boost to a few ex-students who donated to or otherwise helped their college.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Two-series line chart** with **point markers** and a **shaded difference band** (area between lines).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Overlay of two lines** on the same axes.
- **Shading (filled area)** between the lines to emphasize the gap (“estimated legacy boost”).
- No faceting/small multiples; no stacking.

### 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Parents’ income rank (percentile; poorer → richer) | Ordinal | Position on **x-axis**; x-axis tick labels (10th, 30th, 50th, 70th, 90th, 99.9) |
| Admissions rate / likelihood of being admitted (shown as “x” multiples) | Quantitative | Position on **y-axis** (right-side scale labeled 2x, 4x, 6x) |
| Applicant group / scenario (legacy applicants vs. without legacy status) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/line style** (black line vs. gray line) and corresponding labels (“For legacy applicants”, “Estimated advantage without legacy status”) |
| Estimated legacy boost (difference between legacy and non-legacy) | Quantitative (derived) | **Shaded area** between the two lines; annotation label (“Estimated legacy boost”) |
| Highlighted top income group (“Top 0.1%”) | Ordinal (subset of income rank) | **Annotation** near the far-right point (“TOP 0.1% 7x as likely to be admitted”) and emphasized endpoint marker position |

raw_comment:
Notice: I notice that the graph's scale on the x axis isn’t consistent. It is scaled in a way to make it seem like there is more changing than there really is. The graph’s y axis scale seems more consistent and equally spaced out. Legacy applicants are more likely to be accepted into an Ivy League school than the average admission rate of those students who are not legacy applicants. If their parent’s income rank is higher, there are more students accepted into Ivy League schools in general, but much more for legacy applicants.  

Wonder: I wonder why they felt the need to skew the data on the x axis? What benefit does it really do to encourage the idea that legacy applicants have a more likely chance of being admitted into an Ivy League school?

Relate: This data is applicable and similar to other tiers of colleges as well. Numerous college admissions request essays, and the college may be more likely to accept a legacy applicant than one whose parents either went to another institution or didn’t go to college at all. 

Headline: Legacy Student Advantages in Admissions

raw_comment:
Noticed: The chart is carefully crafted to support a kneejerk reaction that rich legacy applicants are getting admitted at the expense of poor applicants. Looking closer, I notice non-legacy applicants below the 40th income percentile have about 1.75 x the average non-legacy admissions rate. These applicants will likely need a lot of financial aid, due to the bloated cost of college. So the colleges accept a larger proportion of legacy applicants, particularly at the higher income levels. The colleges know that statistically, these applicants’ families will donate large amounts, that can be redistributed down to the 40th percentile and below. Legacy admission advantage and lower-income admissions advantage are the yin and yang of college admissions. At first glance, colleges appear to be acting as Robin Hoods, taking from the rich and giving to the poor. But looking deeper, they are really acting as Grifters – Bloating up their staff and salaries, then stealing from the rich to give to the poor, then demanding the poor turn the money over to – the colleges’ bloated staff and salaries!
It's disappointing but not surprising that The NY Times, which sees victimhood everywhere, cannot bring itself to delve deeper into this to provide a comprehensive, fair analysis.

raw_comment:
Noticed: The difference between the estimated legacy boost and without the legacy boost is quite significant, especially since students have the same test scores. Not only does the legacy boost increase the chance of getting into that college, but the parents income rank further strengthens that boost making it extremely difficult for a person without a legacy boost, or parents with high income, to be admitted to some of the more elite colleges. 
Wondered: How many students did they use as data points to get this graph of data? Would the data have changed if the students had different test scores? What where the test scores of the students? Could they have colored coded the lines depending on the different colleges the students where aiming for? 
Relate: This information is very useful for students trying to apply to different colleges, because it shows how depending on your legacy and parents income rank, you have to work really hard if you don't have those 2 things and because I am also aiming for a more competitive college, I am able to observe this information and use it to my advantage when applying for colleges.

Relate: This relates to regular colleges as well. When filling out scholarship applications there is always a question about if your parents or siblings have gone to that specific college. That automatically qualifies you to get a chance at better scholarships.
